content: Just as the human brain relies on reactivated long-term memory traces (rather than isolated short-term buffers) to retain critical information over long horizons of active learning, 2025 ICM-based autonomous agents can replace their standard finite novel state buffers by tapping into a persistent reactivated long-term memory storage to sustain curiosity-driven exploration over extended task horizons. This solves the common problem of ICM agents forgetting previously discovered novel but unprocessed stimuli when exploring for thousands of timesteps.
source_topics: Working memory retention mechanisms for long-horizon active learning
trigger_topic: Working memory retention mechanisms for long-horizon active learning
